choiceresponse.label: Check the Lime explainer output for the fourth sentence: “Delivery update: we couldn’t complete delivery. Confirm your address to avoid a return. Ref: 49281.” This sentence resembles common spam messages, yet is classified as ham. Why? (Select all that apply.)

checkboxgroup.choice: LIME shows “update” and reference-style formatting contributing toward ham.
The model ignores phrases like “confirm your address”.
The presence of a reference number shifts the prediction toward legitimate communication.
The model has learned that delivery-related messages are always ham.
LIME highlights both spam-like and ham-like signals, with ham dominating.

div.p: The LIME explanation indicates that this sentence contains mixed signals. Phrases like “confirm your address” resemble common spam language and do contribute toward the spam class. However, LIME also highlights “update”, the neutral delivery framing, and the inclusion of a structured reference number (“Ref: 49281”) as supporting the ham class. These elements are characteristic of legitimate transactional notifications. The final classification reflects how the model weighs these competing cues, with the legitimacy-related features outweighing the spam-like ones for this specific message.
